title: R posts
text:
Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R


Google’s new OKF turns organizational knowledge into folders of markdown. Here’s how to validate one, load it into a DuckDB catalog, and run semantic search over it — entirely from R.





Jun 22, 2026


Travis Jakel





No matching items

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
In June 2026, Google Cloud published the Open Knowledge Format (OKF) — a vendor-neutral way to represent the curated knowledge that AI agents need as context. The format is refreshingly boring: a directory of markdown files with YAML frontmatter, one “concept” per file, with markdown links forming a graph. If you can cat a file, you can read OKF.
That minimalism is the point — but it leaves a gap. Google shipped a producer (an agent that turns BigQuery tables into OKF) and an HTML visualizer, and the community quickly added validators. What was missing was a way to load an OKF bundle into something you can actually query — and nothing existed in R at all.
So I wrote okf-ingest, an R + Python package that reads a bundle, validates it, loads it into a portable DuckDB catalog, and lets you semantically search it. This post walks the R side end to end. (Disclosure: it’s my package.)

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
section: What an OKF bundle looks like
text:
What an OKF bundle looks like
A concept file is just frontmatter + markdown:
---
type: Metric
title: "Revenue"
description: "Total order value over a period."
tags: [finance, kpi]
timestamp: 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
---

# Revenue

Computed from [orders](/orders.md).
The only field OKF requires is type — and it’s a free string, not an enum, because consumers must tolerate unknown types. Everything else (title, description, tags, timestamp) is recommended. Links like [orders](/orders.md) are untyped edges that turn the folder into a graph.

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
Install
okf is on R-universe:
install.packages("okf", repos = c(
  travisjakel = "https://travisjakel.r-universe.dev",
  CRAN        = "https://cloud.r-project.org"
))

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
Validate, permissively
OKF’s conformance bar is deliberately tiny: every non-reserved .md file must have parseable frontmatter with a non-empty type. Everything else — missing recommended fields, broken links, unknown types — is a finding, never a rejection. okf follows that to the letter:
library(okf)

rd  <- okf_read("store")          # a bundle directory
val <- okf_validate(rd)
val
#>                 path severity        rule                              message
#> 1 metrics/revenue.md     warn broken_link unresolved link: /returns.md
One soft warning (a deliberately broken link in the example bundle), and the bundle is still conformant. A real linter would stop(); OKF says load what you can and report the rest.

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
Load into a DuckDB catalog
okf_ingest() reads, validates, and writes everything into a DuckDB database:
res <- okf_ingest("store", db_path = "kb.duckdb")
str(res$summary)
#> List of 8
#>  $ n_files     : int 5
#>  $ n_concepts  : int 3        # index.md / log.md are reserved, not concepts
#>  $ n_conformant: int 3
#>  $ conformant  : logi TRUE
#>  $ errors      : int 0
#>  $ warnings    : int 1
#>  $ links_total : int 8
#>  $ links_broken: int 1
From here it’s just DuckDB — four tables (okf_bundle, okf_concept, okf_link, okf_validation) you can hit with DBI:
library(DBI)
con <- res$con

dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT path, type, title FROM okf_concept WHERE NOT reserved")
#>                 path           type    title
#> 1       customers.md BigQuery Table Customers
#> 2 metrics/revenue.md         Metric   Revenue
#> 3          orders.md BigQuery Table   Orders

# the concept graph
okf_graph_df(con)[, c("src_path", "dst_path", "resolved")]
Because the catalog is plain DuckDB, it’s queryable from R, Python, or the bare duckdb CLI — and it’s a single file you can ship.

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
The payoff: semantic search
This is where okf-ingest goes past the other tools. okf_embed() chunks each concept body and embeds it (by default with a local Ollama nomic-embed-text model — no data leaves your machine, no API key), storing the vectors right in the catalog:
okf_embed(con)            # -> number of chunks embedded
Then okf_rag() embeds a query and ranks chunks by cosine similarity, using DuckDB’s native list_cosine_similarity — no vector-database extension required:
okf_rag(con, "how is revenue computed?", k = 3)[, c("path", "title", "score")]
#>                 path     title score
#> 1 metrics/revenue.md   Revenue 0.709
#> 2          orders.md    Orders 0.642
#> 3       customers.md Customers 0.478
The pluggable embedder is just a function of texts returning vectors, so swapping in an OpenAI client (or anything else) is a one-liner.

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
Ingest straight from GitHub
You don’t need the bundle on disk. okf_ingest() accepts a git URL or a tar/zip archive (local or remote); it clones/extracts to a temp dir, finds the bundle, and cleans up after itself:
res2 <- okf_ingest(
  "https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog.git",
  subdir  = "okf/samples/bitcoin",     # bundle within the repo
  db_path = "bitcoin.duckdb"
)

title: Querying Google’s Open Knowledge Format from R
text:
Honest scope
OKF is two weeks old — “an invitation, not yet a standard.” And the validate/lint niche already has good tools (a Node validator, a pure-Rust crate). okf-ingest isn’t trying to be another linter. It’s the only tool that turns a bundle into a SQL-queryable, semantically-searchable DuckDB catalog, and the only one with R (and Python) bindings — which is exactly the gap if you live in the tidy/DuckDB/arrow world.
If you’re experimenting with agent context, knowledge bases, or just want a tidy way to query a folder of markdown, give it a spin:

Repo: https://github.com/travisjakel/okf-ingest
OKF spec: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog

Issues and PRs welcome.

title: About
text:
This blog covers the Open Knowledge Format (OKF) and okf-ingest — an open-source tool that reads, validates, and loads OKF bundles into a portable, semantically-searchable DuckDB catalog, from both R and Python.
Written by Travis Jakel. Posts are syndicated on R-bloggers and Planet Python.

title: Make any OKF knowledge bundle searchable: DuckDB + embeddings in Python
text:
If you’re building with AI agents, you’ve felt the context problem: the model is only as good as the curated knowledge you feed it. In June 2026 Google Cloud proposed a standard for exactly that — the Open Knowledge Format (OKF): a vendor-neutral way to represent organizational knowledge as a directory of markdown files with YAML frontmatter. One concept per file, markdown links as a graph, no required tooling. It formalizes the “LLM wiki” pattern teams were already hacking together.
The format is intentionally minimal, which leaves an obvious question: once you have an OKF bundle, how do you query it — or feed it to a retrieval pipeline? Google shipped a producer and a visualizer; the community added validators. What was missing was a loader that makes a bundle queryable and searchable.
okf-ingest fills that gap. It reads a bundle, validates it (permissively, per spec), loads it into a portable DuckDB catalog, and lets you semantically search it — in Python and R, over the same catalog file. (Disclosure: it’s my tool.)

title: Make any OKF knowledge bundle searchable: DuckDB + embeddings in Python
text:
One command, from a URL
Install and ingest a bundle straight from GitHub — no clone, no download dance. The CLI handles git URLs and tar/zip archives, clones/extracts to a temp dir, finds the bundle, and cleans up:
pip install okf-ingest

okf ingest https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog.git \
  --subdir okf/samples/bitcoin --db kb.duckdb
#> ingested ... -> kb.duckdb
#>   concepts=… conformant=… (True) errors=0 warnings=… links=… broken=…
validate is a CI-friendly linter (non-zero exit on non-conformance); query runs SQL or a body search over the catalog:
okf validate ./my-bundle --strict
okf query kb.duckdb --search revenue

title: Make any OKF knowledge bundle searchable: DuckDB + embeddings in Python
text:
In Python
The library mirrors the CLI. ingest() returns an open DuckDB connection plus a summary; from there it’s plain DuckDB:
import okf

con, summary = okf.ingest("./store", db_path="kb.duckdb")
print(summary)
# {'n_files': 5, 'n_concepts': 3, 'n_conformant': 3, 'conformant': True,
#  'errors': 0, 'warnings': 1, 'links_total': 8, 'links_broken': 1}

con.execute(
    "SELECT path, type, title FROM okf_concept WHERE NOT reserved"
).fetchall()
# [('customers.md', 'BigQuery Table', 'Customers'),
#  ('metrics/revenue.md', 'Metric', 'Revenue'),
#  ('orders.md', 'BigQuery Table', 'Orders')]
OKF requires exactly one frontmatter field — type, a free string — and is emphatically permissive: missing recommended fields, unknown types, and broken links become findings, never rejections. The catalog records them in an okf_validation table.

title: Make any OKF knowledge bundle searchable: DuckDB + embeddings in Python
text:
Semantic search, no vector DB
This is the part the other OKF tools don’t do. embed() chunks concept bodies and embeds them (default: a local Ollama nomic-embed-text model — no API key, nothing leaves your machine), storing vectors in the catalog. The embedder is just a callable texts -> list[vector], so swapping in OpenAI is trivial.
import okf.rag as rag

con, _ = okf.ingest("./store", db_path="kb.duckdb")
rag.embed(con)                                   # chunk + embed

for path, title, cid, score, text in rag.rag(con, "how is revenue computed?", k=3):
    print(f"[{score:.3f}] {path} — {title}")
# [0.709] metrics/revenue.md — Revenue
# [0.642] orders.md — Orders
# [0.478] customers.md — Customers
Search is brute-force cosine via DuckDB’s native list_cosine_similarity — no vector-database extension required. For most knowledge bundles (hundreds to tens of thousands of chunks) that’s plenty; a DuckDB-vss HNSW index is a drop-in upgrade for larger corpora.

title: Make any OKF knowledge bundle searchable: DuckDB + embeddings in Python
text:
The reusable pattern
Strip away OKF and the takeaway is a portable RAG index over a folder of files: parse → load into DuckDB → embed → cosine-search, all in one self-contained .duckdb file with zero services to run. Because the catalog format is shared across the bindings, you can ingest in Python and query in R (or vice-versa) — same file, same tables.

title: Make any OKF knowledge bundle searchable: DuckDB + embeddings in Python
text:
Honest scope
OKF is brand new and may evolve, and the validator niche already has solid tools (Node, a pure-Rust crate). okf-ingest is for the next step: turning a bundle into a queryable, searchable catalog from Python or R. If you’re wiring OKF into a retrieval pipeline or an agent’s context, it should save you an afternoon.

Repo: https://github.com/travisjakel/okf-ingest
OKF spec: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog

Stars, issues, and PRs welcome.
